topic.what: Landmark 1949 case series demonstrating that fresh cabbage juice healed peptic ulcers in an average of 10.4 days compared to 37 days with standard therapy, attributing the effect to a then-unidentified factor later called 'vitamin U'.
topic.how: Prospective case series (n=13) at Stanford University Hospital: patients consumed one liter of fresh cabbage juice daily; healing confirmed by radiological and symptomatic assessment; outcomes compared to historical control healing times from the literature.
topic.why_matters: Provided the first clinical evidence that a dietary factor in raw cabbage (subsequently identified as S-methylmethionine) could dramatically accelerate peptic ulcer healing, opening a research line into dietary mucosal protection relevant to gastritis.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Average days to confirmed peptic ulcer healing with cabbage juice treatment
characteristics.vs_baseline: 37 days with standard dietary/medical therapy (historical control)
characteristics.evidence: Case series of 13 patients; radiological confirmation of healing; published in California Medicine 1949
characteristics.context: Historical controls from literature rather than concurrent RCT; small sample; 1949 standard of care differs from modern practice

characteristics.value: Uncontrolled case series with historical comparators; no randomization or blinding
characteristics.evidence: Single-site, single-investigator study; Garnett Cheney, Stanford University
characteristics.context: Findings historically influential but require replication with modern methodology

good_for: Generating hypotheses about cruciferous vegetable compounds and gastric mucosal protection
Historical context for S-methylmethionine (vitamin U) research
Naturopathic dietary protocols for peptic ulcer and gastritis
not_for: Evidence-based clinical guidelines without modern RCT replication
H. pylori-positive ulcer disease where antibiotic eradication is indicated
requires: Replication in modern RCT with concurrent controls and endoscopic outcome confirmation
Mechanistic studies on S-methylmethionine mucosal protection pathways

novelty: First clinical evidence that a dietary intervention (cabbage juice) could markedly accelerate peptic ulcer healing
Identified existence of a protective dietary factor distinct from known vitamins, later named 'vitamin U'
open_problems: Modern RCT replication of cabbage juice efficacy in peptic ulcer and gastritis has not been conducted
Mechanisms by which S-methylmethionine protects gastric mucosa remain incompletely characterized
Optimal dose and preparation of cabbage juice for therapeutic effect is undefined
